title: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone
authors: Rajesh K. Jain, Tamara J. Vokes

items.subtopic: Paget's Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone | PAGET'S DISEASE OF BONE (part 1)
items.title: Epidemiology and Prevalence of Paget's Disease
items.content: Paget's disease shows marked geographic variation, with higher prevalence in Western Europe and among immigrants to certain regions. The disease is rare in native populations outside these areas unless there is European ancestry. Its prevalence is decreasing, and the age at diagnosis is increasing.
keyPoints: High prevalence in Western Europe (Great Britain, France, Germany) and among immigrants to Australia, New Zealand, South Africa, North and South America.
Rare in native populations of Americas, Africa, Asia, Middle East unless European ancestry is present.
Prevalence decreases over time; age at diagnosis increases.
Autopsy series reveal Paget's disease in ~3% of those over age 40.
Positive skeletal radiographs prevalence: 2.5% for men and 1.6% for women aged >55 years.
Elevated ALP levels incidence: 12.7 per 100,000 person-years in men, 7 in women.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · Paget's Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone | PAGET'S DISEASE OF BONE (part 1)

items.subtopic: Paget's Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone | PAGET'S DISEASE OF BONE (part 1)
items.title: Genetic Etiology of Paget's Disease
items.content: Paget's disease has a genetic component, with familial cases showing autosomal dominant inheritance. Several genes are implicated in both familial and sporadic forms.
keyPoints: Positive family history in 15–25% of patients increases prevalence seven- to tenfold among first-degree relatives.
Genetic basis established for several rare familial bone disorders resembling Paget's disease.
Juvenile Paget's disease linked to TNFRSF11B gene mutation (osteoprotegerin).
Familial patterns suggest autosomal dominant inheritance with variable penetrance.
Mutations in SQSTM1, CSF1, RIN3, OPTN, TNFRSF11A, TM7SF4 genes implicated.
SQSTM1 mutations show phenotypic variability suggesting other genetic or viral influences.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · Paget's Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone | PAGET'S DISEASE OF BONE (part 1)

items.subtopic: Paget's Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone | PAGET'S DISEASE OF BONE (part 1)
items.title: Viral Etiology Hypothesis in Paget's Disease
items.content: Evidence suggests a potential viral contribution to Paget's disease, though it remains controversial. The decline in incidence coincides with measles vaccination.
keyPoints: Cytoplasmic and nuclear inclusions resembling paramyxoviruses found in pagetic osteoclasts.
Viral mRNA present in precursor and mature osteoclasts.
Osteoclast precursors convert to pagetic-like cells via vectors with measles virus genes.
Incidence decline coincides with widespread measles vaccination.
Controversy exists due to inability to culture live virus or clone full-length viral genes from patient samples.
No higher antibody levels against paramyxoviruses in patients compared to controls.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · Paget's Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone | PAGET'S DISEASE OF BONE (part 1)

items.title: Understanding the Pathophysiology of Paget's Disease
items.content: Paget's disease is characterized by an increased number and activity of osteoclasts, leading to enhanced bone resorption. This abnormality results from several factors including hypersensitivity to 1,25(OH)₂D₃, hyperresponsiveness to RANK ligand (RANKL), and overexpression of c-fos and Bcl-2 genes.
keyPoints: Osteoclasts are increased 10- to 100-fold in number with up to 100 nuclei compared to normal 3–5 nuclei.
Resorptive surfaces may increase sevenfold, with erosion rates rising from 1 μg/d to 9 μg/d.
Increased osteoclastic activity is mediated by factors such as hypersensitivity to 1,25(OH)₂D₃ and hyperresponsiveness to RANKL.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · PAGET'S DISEASE OF BONE (part 2)

items.title: Clinical Manifestations of Paget's Disease
items.content: Paget's disease often presents asymptomatically, detected through elevated ALP levels or radiographic abnormalities. Common symptoms include bone pain, fractures, and deformities due to increased vascularity and lytic lesions.
keyPoints: Diagnosis is frequently incidental in asymptomatic patients with elevated ALP levels.
Commonly affected skeletal sites include the pelvis, vertebral bodies, skull, femur, and tibia.
Symptoms may involve pain from increased vascularity or fractures, bowing of long bones, and neurologic complications from cranial involvement.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · PAGET'S DISEASE OF BONE (part 2)

items.title: Complications and Management in Paget's Disease
items.content: Paget's disease can lead to serious complications such as fractures, neoplasms, and cardiovascular issues. Fractures typically occur at active lytic sites, while osteosarcomas are rare but possible.
keyPoints: Fractures commonly occur in the femoral shaft and subtrochanteric regions.
Neoplasms like osteosarcoma arise rarely (<0.5%) and usually present with new pain.
Cardiovascular complications include high-output heart failure, associated with extensive skeletal involvement (15–35% of skeleton) and elevated ALP levels.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · PAGET'S DISEASE OF BONE (part 2)

items.title: Diagnosis of Paget's Disease
items.content: Paget's disease is diagnosed through clinical examination, radiologic findings, and biochemical markers. Clinical signs include enlarged skull with frontal bossing, bowing extremities, or simian posturing. Radiographic features are characteristic, such as cortical thickening and 'cotton wool' appearance in the skull. Biochemical evaluation involves monitoring bone turnover using serum total ALP and other markers.
keyPoints: Clinical signs: enlarged skull with frontal bossing, bowing extremities, simian posturing.
Radiographic findings: cortical thickening, coarsening of trabecular markings, 'cotton wool' appearance in the skull.
Biochemical markers: serum total ALP is the test of choice for diagnosis and monitoring response to therapy.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · PAGET'S DISEASE OF BONE (part 3)

items.title: Imaging Modalities in Paget's Disease
items.content: Radiographs are essential for diagnosing Paget's disease, showing typical bone changes. Bone scans with $^{99m}$Tc are more sensitive than radiographs for detecting active lesions. CT and MRI are not routinely necessary but can be useful for assessing fractures or malignancy risks in pagetic bones.
keyPoints: Radiographs show characteristic bone changes such as cortical thickening and 'ivory vertebra'.
$^{99m}$Tc bone scans are more sensitive than radiographs for active lesions.
CT is useful for fracture assessment; MRI is necessary for evaluating sarcoma or metastatic disease risk.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · PAGET'S DISEASE OF BONE (part 3)

items.title: Biochemical Evaluation and Management of Paget's Disease
items.content: Biochemical markers are crucial in diagnosing and managing Paget's disease. Serum total ALP is the primary marker for diagnosis and monitoring therapy response. Bone-specific ALP, P1NP, and bone resorption markers like N-telopeptide or C-telopeptide also provide insights into disease activity. Hypercalcemia can occur with immobilization, while hypocalcemia may develop during bisphosphonate therapy.
keyPoints: Serum total ALP is the primary marker for diagnosis and monitoring.
Bone-specific ALP and P1NP reflect disease activity; bone resorption markers decrease rapidly in response to therapy.
Hypercalcemia can occur with immobilization; hypocalcemia may develop during bisphosphonate therapy.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · PAGET'S DISEASE OF BONE (part 3)

items.title: Indications for Pharmacologic Therapy in Paget's Disease
items.content: Pharmacologic therapy is recommended for most patients with active Paget's disease who are at risk of complications, even if asymptomatic. Treatment aims to control symptoms and prevent complications associated with metabolically active disease.
keyPoints: Treatment indicated for symptomatic patients or those at risk of complications.

Goals include controlling bone pain, fractures, headaches, radiculopathy, arthropathy, neurologic complications, reducing operative blood loss, hypercalciuria during immobilization, and preventing complications in high-risk sites (elevated ALP, weight-bearing bones, major joints, vertebral bodies, skull).
Randomized studies showed no difference in pain, fracture rates, quality of life, or hearing loss between symptomatic treatment and normalization of serum ALP.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · Paget's Disease of Bone

items.title: First-Line Pharmacologic Treatment for Paget's Disease
items.content: Zoledronic acid is the first-choice bisphosphonate for severe Paget's disease or rapid normalization needs due to its efficacy in normalizing bone turnover.
keyPoints: Zoledronic acid recommended as first choice, especially for severe disease or rapid normalization required (neurologic symptoms, severe pain, risk of fracture, pre-surgery).
Normalizes bone turnover in >90% of patients; effect persists months to years.
Administered at 5 mg intravenous infusion over 20 min; slower rates recommended for elderly or mild renal impairment.
Contraindicated with significant renal impairment (GFR <35 mL/min).
Approximately 20-25% experience a flulike syndrome post-first infusion, mitigated by pretreatment.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · Paget's Disease of Bone

items.title: Alternative Treatments for Paget's Disease
items.content: Oral bisphosphonates and other agents offer alternatives when zoledronic acid is contraindicated or not tolerated.
keyPoints: Alendronate and risedronate are oral options for mild disease or renal impairment; taken on empty stomach, upright posture maintained post-dose.
Salmon calcitonin subcutaneous form rarely used due to low potency, reserved for those intolerant of bisphosphonates.
Denosumab (RANKL antibody) reduces ALP but is not approved and less effective/durable than bisphosphonates.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · Paget's Disease of Bone

items.title: Understanding Osteopetrosis: Types and Genetic Basis
items.content: Osteopetrosis is characterized by impaired osteoclast-mediated bone resorption, leading to increased bone density. It includes malignant (severe, infantile) and benign (adult) forms, with genetic mutations in CA II, TCIRG1, and CLCN7 playing significant roles.
keyPoints: Osteopetrosis results from impaired osteoclast-mediated bone resorption.
Types include malignant (autosomal recessive) and benign (autosomal dominant types I and II).
Genetic mutations: CA II deficiency, TCIRG1 gene mutation, CLCN7 chloride channel gene mutation.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · OSTEOPETROSIS

items.title: Clinical Presentation of Osteopetrosis: Incidence and Symptoms
items.content: Autosomal recessive severe osteopetrosis is rare, with an incidence of 1 in 200,000 to 1 in 500,000 live births. It can lead to cranial nerve paralysis, extramedullary hematopoiesis, pancytopenia, and hypocalcemia, often being fatal before age 5.
keyPoints: Incidence of severe osteopetrosis: 1 in 200,000 to 1 in 500,000 live births.
Symptoms include cranial nerve paralysis, extramedullary hematopoiesis, pancytopenia, and hypocalcemia.
Untreated infantile disease is often fatal before age 5.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · OSTEOPETROSIS

items.title: Radiographic Features of Osteopetrosis: Diagnostic Imaging
items.content: Radiography in osteopetrosis shows generalized symmetric increases in bone mass, thickening of cortical and trabecular bone, broadened diaphyses and metaphyses, and underpneumatized sinuses.
keyPoints: Radiographic features include increased bone mass and thickened cortical/trabecular bone.
Diaphyses and metaphyses are broadened with alternating sclerotic/lucent bands.
Thickened cranium base and underpneumatized paranasal/mastoid sinuses.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · OSTEOPETROSIS

items.title: Bone Marrow Transplantation in Osteopetrosis: Timing and Outcomes
items.content: Allogeneic HLA-identical bone marrow transplantation can be successful in children with osteopetrosis, particularly when performed before age 4. This procedure introduces normally functioning osteoclasts into the marrow, leading to radiographic improvements over time.
keyPoints: Transplantation is most likely curative if done before age 4.
Radiographic improvements include changes in Erlenmeyer flask deformities but not complete normalization.
Marrow transplantation from nonidentical HLA-matched donors has a higher failure rate.
Other treatments like interferon $ \\$gamma $-1 $ \\beta $, 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D, methylprednisolone, and dietary adjustments have shown variable benefits.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · Osteopetrosis | PYKNODYSOSTOSIS

items.title: Clinical Features and Management of Pyknodysostosis
items.content: Pyknodysostosis is an autosomal recessive osteosclerosis characterized by mutations in the cathepsin K gene, leading to nonfunctional osteoclasts. It presents with distinct clinical features and requires specific management strategies.
keyPoints: Clinical features include short stature, kyphoscoliosis, high arched palate, proptosis, blue sclerae, dysmorphic facial features, small square hands, and hypoplastic nails.
Radiographs show increased bone density with normally shaped long bones; separated cranial sutures are characteristic.
Histologic evaluation reveals normal cortical architecture but decreased osteoblastic and osteoclastic activities.
Serum chemistries are normal, and there is no anemia unlike in osteopetrosis.
No known treatment exists for pyknodysostosis; bone marrow transplantation has not been attempted.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · Osteopetrosis | PYKNODYSOSTOSIS

items.subtopic: PROGRESSIVE DIAPHYSEAL DYSPLASIA | HYPEROSTOSIS CORTICALIS GENERALISATA
items.title: Recognizing and Managing Progressive Diaphyseal Dysplasia (Camurati-Engelmann Disease)
items.content: Progressive diaphyseal dysplasia, also known as Camurati-Engelmann disease, is an autosomal dominant disorder characterized by diaphyseal hyperostosis. It involves symmetric thickening of the endosteal and periosteal surfaces in long bones like the femur and tibia. The genetic defect lies on chromosome 19q13.2 affecting TGF-$\beta1$, leading to its activation. Clinically, patients present with pain, tenderness, fatigue, muscle wasting, and gait disturbances. Radiographic findings include new bone formation along diaphyses, while bone scintigraphy shows increased radiotracer uptake. Treatment options include low-dose glucocorticoids for symptom relief and intermittent bisphosphonate therapy.
keyPoints: Autosomal dominant disorder
Chromosome 19q13.2 mutation affecting TGF-$\beta1$
Symmetric thickening of long bones' diaphyses, particularly femur and tibia
Clinical symptoms: pain, fatigue, muscle wasting, gait disturbance
Radiographic findings: patchy endosteal and periosteal new bone formation
Bone scintigraphy: increased radiotracer uptake in affected areas
Treatment: low-dose glucocorticoids, intermittent bisphosphonate therapy
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · PROGRESSIVE DIAPHYSEAL DYSPLASIA | HYPEROSTOSIS CORTICALIS GENERALISATA

items.subtopic: PROGRESSIVE DIAPHYSEAL DYSPLASIA | HYPEROSTOSIS CORTICALIS GENERALISATA
items.title: Understanding Hyperostosis Corticalis Generalisata (van Buchem's Disease)
items.content: Hyperostosis corticalis generalisata, or van Buchem's disease, is an autosomal recessive disorder characterized by endosteal hyperostosis and osteosclerosis affecting the skull, mandible, clavicles, and ribs. It leads to narrowed cranial foramens causing neural compressions with potential optic atrophy, facial paralysis, and deafness. Adults may have an enlarged mandible, and serum ALP levels are often elevated due to uncoupled bone remodeling. The condition is linked to mutations in the SOST gene.
keyPoints: Autosomal recessive disorder
Endosteal hyperostosis with osteosclerosis of skull, mandible, clavicles, ribs
Neural compressions may cause optic atrophy, facial paralysis, deafness
Elevated serum ALP levels due to high osteoblastic formation and low osteoclastic resorption
Linked to mutations in the SOST gene
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · PROGRESSIVE DIAPHYSEAL DYSPLASIA | HYPEROSTOSIS CORTICALIS GENERALISATA

items.title: Understanding Melorheostosis: Clinical Presentation and Genetic Basis
items.content: Melorheostosis presents as progressive linear hyperostosis, typically in a lower extremity, with symptoms of pain or stiffness appearing during childhood. Radiographs show a characteristic 'melted wax' appearance. Somatic mutations in MAP2K1 and SMAD3 are implicated.
keyPoints: Progressive linear hyperostosis usually affects one limb, often a lower extremity.
Radiographic appearance resembles melted wax dripping down a candle.
Symptoms include pain or stiffness during childhood; disease does not progress in adults.
Somatic mutations identified: MAP2K1 (increases MEK1 activity) and SMAD3 (upregulates TGF-β/SMAD pathway).
No specific treatment available; surgical interventions often unsuccessful.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · MELORHEOSTOSIS | OSTEOPOIKILOSIS | HEPATITIS C-ASSOCIATED OSTEOSCLEROSIS

items.title: Distinguishing Osteopoikilosis: Clinical Features and Genetic Insights
items.content: Osteopoikilosis is a benign condition characterized by multiple small foci of bony sclerosis, stable over time. It can be confused with metastatic lesions but does not accumulate radionucleotide on bone scanning. Mutations in LEMD3 are commonly involved.
keyPoints: Benign autosomal dominant condition with numerous small, variably shaped foci of bony sclerosis.
Lesions stable over time; do not accumulate radionucleotide on bone scanning.
Commonly involves bones except skull, ribs, and vertebrae.
May be associated with dermatofibrosis lenticularis disseminata (Buschke-Ollendorff syndrome).
Caused by mutations in LEMD3, affecting BMP signaling.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · MELORHEOSTOSIS | OSTEOPOIKILOSIS | HEPATITIS C-ASSOCIATED OSTEOSCLEROSIS

items.title: Recognizing Hypophosphatasia: Clinical Presentation and Laboratory Findings
items.content: Hypophosphatasia is a rare inherited disorder characterized by low serum levels of alkaline phosphatase (ALP) despite rickets in children or osteomalacia in adults. Clinically, it presents with failure to thrive, rachitic deformities, craniosynostosis, and flail chest in infants, while adults may experience stress fractures and muscle pain.
keyPoints: Hypophosphatasia is rare, with severe forms occurring at a frequency of about 1 in 100,000 live births in Canada.
Clinical manifestations vary from intrauterine death to premature tooth loss.
Low ALP levels are diagnostic despite normal or elevated serum calcium and phosphorus.
Specific elevation of pyridoxal 5'-phosphate (PLP) is a hallmark of the disease.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · HYPOPHOSPHATASIA

items.title: Management Considerations in Hypophosphatasia: Avoiding Harmful Treatments
items.content: In hypophosphatasia, avoid calcium and vitamin D supplementation as they can worsen hypercalcemia and hypercalciuria. Instead, consider a low-calcium diet, glucocorticoids, or calcitonin for management.
keyPoints: Calcium and vitamin D should be avoided to prevent aggravating hypercalcemia and hypercalciuria.
Alternative treatments include low-calcium diets, glucocorticoids, and calcitonin.
Fracture healing is poor; intramedullary rods are preferred for fracture repair.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · HYPOPHOSPHATASIA

items.title: Therapeutic Advances in Hypophosphatasia: Enzyme Replacement Therapy
items.content: Asfotase alfa, a tissue nonspecific alkaline phosphatase (TNSALP), is an approved enzyme replacement therapy for perinatal/infantile and juvenile-onset hypophosphatasia. It has shown sustained improvements in mineralization and functional abilities.
keyPoints: Asfotase alfa was approved in 2015 for enzyme replacement therapy.
Improvements observed include enhanced mineralization, respiratory function, growth, and increased 6-min walk time.
Therapy duration of 7 years shows sustained benefits in children; 5 years in adolescents/adults.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · HYPOPHOSPHATASIA

items.title: Axial Osteomalacia: Recognize the Benign Course and Ineffective Treatments
items.content: Axial osteomalacia is a rare disorder marked by defective skeletal mineralization despite normal serum calcium and phosphate levels. It typically presents in middle-aged or elderly men with chronic axial skeletal discomfort, often including cervical spine pain. Radiographic findings show osteosclerosis due to coarsened trabecular patterns typical of osteomalacia, affecting the spine, pelvis, and ribs most commonly. The primary defect is an acquired dysfunction in osteoblasts, leading to defective mineralization. The condition follows a benign course with no established treatment, as calcium and vitamin D therapies are ineffective.
keyPoints: Rare disorder characterized by defective skeletal mineralization despite normal serum calcium and phosphate levels
Presents in middle-aged or elderly men with chronic axial skeletal discomfort
Radiographic findings: osteosclerosis due to coarsened trabecular patterns typical of osteomalacia, commonly affecting spine, pelvis, and ribs
Histologic changes show defective mineralization and flat, inactive osteoblasts
Primary defect is an acquired dysfunction in osteoblast function
Benign course with no established treatment; calcium and vitamin D therapies are ineffective
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · AXIAL OSTEOMALACIA | FIBROGENESIS IMPERFECTA OSSIUM

items.title: Fibrogenesis Imperfecta Ossium: Identify the Progressive Course and Potential Growth Hormone Use
items.content: Fibrogenesis imperfecta ossium is a rare condition of unknown etiology, presenting in both sexes typically in middle age or later. It manifests with progressive, intractable skeletal pain, fractures, worsening immobilization, and a debilitating course. The only biochemical abnormality noted is elevated alkaline phosphatase (ALP). Radiographic evaluation reveals generalized osteomalacia, osteopenia, and occasional pseudofractures, while histologic features show a tangled pattern of collagen fibrils with abundant osteoblasts and osteoclasts. Growth hormone use has led to substantial short-term clinical improvement in two adult patients, though long-term outcomes are unknown. No other effective treatment is known, although spontaneous remission has been reported in a small number of cases.
keyPoints: Rare condition with unknown etiology
Presents in both sexes typically in middle age or later
Manifests with progressive, intractable skeletal pain and fractures
Biochemical abnormality: elevated alkaline phosphatase (ALP)
Radiographic findings: generalized osteomalacia, osteopenia, occasional pseudofractures
Histologic features: tangled pattern of collagen fibrils with abundant osteoblasts and osteoclasts
Growth hormone use led to short-term clinical improvement in two adult patients; long-term outcomes unknown
No other effective treatment known; spontaneous remission reported in a small number of cases
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · AXIAL OSTEOMALACIA | FIBROGENESIS IMPERFECTA OSSIUM

items.title: Understanding the Genetic and Clinical Spectrum of Fibrous Dysplasia and McCune-Albright Syndrome
items.content: Fibrous dysplasia is a sporadic disorder characterized by fibrous skeletal lesions due to mutations in the GNAS1 gene, affecting bone-forming mesenchyme. The polyostotic form associated with café au lait spots and endocrine hyperfunction defines McCune-Albright syndrome (MAS). These mutations lead to constitutive activation of the cyclic AMP-protein kinase A pathway, causing autonomous function in various tissues.
keyPoints: Fibrous dysplasia involves one or more expanding fibrous skeletal lesions.
Polyostotic form associated with MAS includes café au lait spots and endocrine hyperfunction such as pseudoprecocious puberty.
Mutations occur in the GNAS1 gene affecting the α subunit of the stimulatory G protein (Gα).
Constitutive stimulation of cyclic AMP-protein kinase A pathway due to mutations.
Autonomous function can affect bone, skin, and various endocrine glands.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · FIBROUS DYSPLASIA AND MCCUNE-ALBRIGHT SYNDROME

items.title: Clinical Presentation and Demographics of Fibrous Dysplasia and MAS
items.content: Monostotic fibrous dysplasia is most common, diagnosed in patients aged 20-30 without skin lesions. Polyostotic form appears in children <10 years old and may progress with age or estrogen exposure. MAS more commonly presents with pseudoprecocious puberty in girls (10:1 ratio).
keyPoints: Monostotic fibrous dysplasia is most common, diagnosed between ages 20-30.
Polyostotic form typically manifests in children <10 years old.
MAS with pseudoprecocious puberty more common in girls (10:1 ratio).
Lesions may become quiescent during puberty and progress during pregnancy or estrogen therapy.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · FIBROUS DYSPLASIA AND MCCUNE-ALBRIGHT SYNDROME

items.title: Radiographic and Laboratory Findings in Fibrous Dysplasia and MAS
items.content: Radiographically, fibrous dysplastic lesions appear as well-defined, radiolucent areas with a ground-glass appearance. Involvement of facial bones may present as leonine facies. Serum ALP can be elevated; calcium, parathyroid hormone, 25-hydroxyvitamin D, and 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D levels are normal. Extensive polyostotic lesions may cause hypophosphatemia due to FGF23.
keyPoints: Radiographic findings: well-defined, radiolucent areas with ground-glass appearance in long bones.
Facial bone involvement can create a leonine facies.
Serum ALP occasionally elevated; calcium and other related levels normal.
Hypophosphatemia and hyperphosphaturia linked to FGF23 in extensive polyostotic lesions.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · FIBROUS DYSPLASIA AND MCCUNE-ALBRIGHT SYNDROME

items.title: Management of Fibrous Dysplasia: Bisphosphonates and Denosumab
items.content: IV bisphosphonate therapy can lead to improvement in bone pain and partial or complete resolution of radiographic lesions. Monthly or quarterly denosumab is effective in reducing bone turnover markers, though it may cause hypercalcemia upon discontinuation.
keyPoints: Bisphosphonates improve bone pain and resolve some radiographic lesions.
Denosumab reduces bone turnover markers; risk of hypercalcemia on discontinuation.
Surgical stabilization is necessary for preventing pathologic fractures or nerve compression.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · Fibrous Dysplasia and MAS | PACHYDERMOPERIOSTOSIS | OSTEOCHONDRODYSPLASIAS

items.title: Distinguishing Primary from Secondary Pachydermoperiostosis
items.content: Primary pachydermoperiostosis, an autosomal dominant disorder, presents with periosteal new bone formation and is differentiated by irregular periosteal surfaces on radiography. It must be distinguished from secondary forms associated with pulmonary disorders.
keyPoints: Autosomal dominant inheritance pattern.
Primary form has irregular periosteal surface; secondary form has smooth surface.
Colchicine may help control arthralgias.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · Fibrous Dysplasia and MAS | PACHYDERMOPERIOSTOSIS | OSTEOCHONDRODYSPLASIAS

items.title: Achondrodysplasia: Genetic Cause and Treatment Options
items.content: Achondrodysplasia, a common form of short-limb dwarfism, is caused by FGFR3 gene mutations. Vosoritide has been approved to increase growth in children, while infigratinib is under clinical trials.
keyPoints: Occurs in 1 in 15,000 to 1 in 40,000 live births.
Caused by gain-of-function mutation of FGFR3 gene.
Vosoritide approved for growth increase; infigratinib in trials.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · Fibrous Dysplasia and MAS | PACHYDERMOPERIOSTOSIS | OSTEOCHONDRODYSPLASIAS

items.subtopic: EXTRASKELETAL (ECTOPIC) CALCIFICATION AND OSSIFICATION | METASTATIC CALCIFICATION | TUMORAL CALCINOSIS
items.title: Mechanisms of Extraskeletal Calcification and Ossification
items.content: Extraskeletal calcification or ossification can occur through three mechanisms: metastatic calcification due to elevated calcium × phosphate concentration product, dystrophic calcification in damaged tissue with normal serum levels, and ectopic ossification involving true bone formation.
keyPoints: Metastatic calcification occurs when the serum calcium × phosphate concentration product is >75.
Dystrophic calcification involves mineral deposition into metabolically impaired or dead tissue despite normal serum calcium and phosphate levels.
Ectopic ossification refers to true bone formation in nonosseous soft tissues.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · EXTRASKELETAL (ECTOPIC) CALCIFICATION AND OSSIFICATION | METASTATIC CALCIFICATION | TUMORAL CALCINOSIS

items.subtopic: EXTRASKELETAL (ECTOPIC) CALCIFICATION AND OSSIFICATION | METASTATIC CALCIFICATION | TUMORAL CALCINOSIS
items.title: Characteristics and Management of Tumoral Calcinosis
items.content: Tumoral calcinosis is a rare genetic disorder characterized by periarticular masses containing hydroxyapatite or amorphous calcium phosphate. It results from mutations in GALNT3, FGF23, or α-Klotho leading to increased renal reabsorption of phosphate and elevated serum phosphate levels.
keyPoints: Tumoral calcinosis often presents in childhood with painless calcific masses near major joints like shoulders, hips, and ankles.
Common laboratory findings include hyperphosphatemia and elevated serum 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D levels, while serum calcium, parathyroid hormone, and ALP are usually normal.
Small deposits may be detected by ^99mTc bone scanning.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · EXTRASKELETAL (ECTOPIC) CALCIFICATION AND OSSIFICATION | METASTATIC CALCIFICATION | TUMORAL CALCINOSIS

items.subtopic: EXTRASKELETAL (ECTOPIC) CALCIFICATION AND OSSIFICATION | METASTATIC CALCIFICATION | TUMORAL CALCINOSIS
items.title: Differential Diagnosis and Red Flags for Metastatic Calcification
items.content: Metastatic calcification can occur in hypercalcemia, hyperphosphatemia, or both. It is commonly seen in renal failure and hemodialysis due to disturbances in calcium and phosphate metabolism.
keyPoints: Soft tissue calcification may be induced by vitamin D and phosphate treatments or calcium administration during hemodialysis.
Calcifications in hypercalcemic states with normal or low phosphate typically affect the kidneys, lungs, and gastric mucosa.
Hyperphosphatemia with normal or low serum calcium can lead to soft tissue calcification, particularly affecting the kidney and arteries.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · EXTRASKELETAL (ECTOPIC) CALCIFICATION AND OSSIFICATION | METASTATIC CALCIFICATION | TUMORAL CALCINOSIS

items.subtopic: Tumoral Calcinosis | DYSTROPHIC CALCIFICATION | ECTOPIC OSSIFICATION | FIBRODYSPLASIA OSSIFICANS PROGRESSIVA
items.title: Management of Tumoral Calcinosis: Surgical and Medical Approaches
items.content: Surgical removal is effective for subcutaneous calcified masses in tumoral calcinosis, provided all calcification is excised to prevent recurrence. Medical management includes reducing serum phosphate through dietary phosphorus restriction or oral phosphate binders, with potential adjunctive use of acetazolamide.
keyPoints: Surgical removal prevents recurrence if complete
Reduce serum phosphate via diet and phosphate binders
Acetazolamide may enhance phosphaturic action

items.title: Fibrodysplasia Ossificans Progressiva: Diagnosis and Emerging Therapies
items.content: Fibrodysplasia ossificans progressiva, a rare autosomal dominant disorder, involves congenital deformities and progressive heterotopic bone formation. Recent therapies include palovarotene, approved for specific age groups in the U.S., reducing new ossification by 60%, though it may cause premature epiphyseal closure.
keyPoints: Rare autosomal dominant disorder
Palovarotene reduces heterotopic ossification by 60%
Approved for females over 8 and males over 10 in the U.S.

items.subtopic: Paget's Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone | PAGET'S DISEASE OF BONE (part 1)
items.question: Which gene mutation is associated with juvenile Paget's disease, also known as familial idiopathic hyperphosphatasia?
items.explanation: A homozygous deletion of the TNFRSF11B gene, which encodes osteoprotegerin, causes juvenile Paget's disease.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · Paget's Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone | PAGET'S DISEASE OF BONE (part 1)

items.subtopic: Paget's Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone | PAGET'S DISEASE OF BONE (part 1)
items.question: Which statement best describes the geographic distribution of Paget's disease?
options: It is most prevalent in Asia and Africa.
It has a high prevalence in Western Europe and among immigrants to Australia, New Zealand, South Africa, and North and South America.
The disease is equally common worldwide.
items.explanation: Paget's disease shows marked geographic variation with high prevalence in Western Europe and among immigrants to certain regions.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · Paget's Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone | PAGET'S DISEASE OF BONE (part 1)

items.subtopic: Paget's Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone | PAGET'S DISEASE OF BONE (part 1)
items.question: What is the primary pathologic process initiating Paget's disease?
options: Overactive osteoclastic bone resorption followed by increased osteoblastic new bone formation.
items.explanation: Paget's disease is initiated by overactive osteoclastic bone resorption followed by compensatory osteoblastic activity.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · Paget's Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone | PAGET'S DISEASE OF BONE (part 1)

items.subtopic: Paget's Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone | PAGET'S DISEASE OF BONE (part 1)
items.question: Which gene is most commonly mutated in both familial and sporadic cases of Paget's disease?
items.explanation: The SQSTM1 gene is the most common mutation identified in familial and sporadic cases of Paget's disease.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · Paget's Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone | PAGET'S DISEASE OF BONE (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following factors is NOT identified as a cause for increased number and activity of pagetic osteoclasts?
options: Increased expression of the proto-oncogene c-fos.
Decreased RANKL expression by marrow stromal cells.
Overexpression of the antiapoptotic oncogene Bcl-2.
items.explanation: Marrow stromal cells from pagetic lesions have increased, not decreased, RANKL expression.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · PAGET'S DISEASE OF BONE (part 2)

items.question: What is the characteristic feature of Paget's disease?
options: Decreased bone resorption with reduced bone formation.
Increased bone resorption accompanied by accelerated bone formation.
Normal bone turnover with decreased bone mass.
Reduced bony vascularity and normal bone structure.
items.explanation: Paget's disease is characterized by increased bone resorption and accelerated bone formation.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · PAGET'S DISEASE OF BONE (part 2)

items.question: Which skeletal site is NOT commonly involved in Paget's disease?
items.explanation: The pelvis, skull, and femur are among the most commonly involved sites; the humerus is not specifically mentioned.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · PAGET'S DISEASE OF BONE (part 2)

items.question: What phase of Paget's disease involves the replacement of normal lamellar bone with haphazard woven bone?
items.explanation: The second phase involves very active bone formation and resorption, replacing normal lamellar bone with woven bone.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · PAGET'S DISEASE OF BONE (part 2)

items.question: Which radiographic finding is typical for Paget's disease and involves the appearance of a 'picture frame' vertebra?
options: Vertebral cortical thickening of the superior and inferior end plates
Diffuse radiodense enlargement of a vertebra
Enlargement or expansion of an entire bone or area of a long bone
items.explanation: Vertebral cortical thickening of the superior and inferior end plates creates a 'picture frame' vertebra.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · PAGET'S DISEASE OF BONE (part 3)

items.question: Which biochemical marker is recommended for monitoring the activity of Paget's disease instead of total ALP?
items.explanation: P1NP reflects the activity of Paget's disease and can be used instead of total ALP.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · PAGET'S DISEASE OF BONE (part 3)

items.question: What is a potential complication of immobilization in patients with active Paget's disease?
items.explanation: Immobilization may rarely cause hypercalcemia and hypercalciuria in active Paget's disease.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · PAGET'S DISEASE OF BONE (part 3)

items.question: Which imaging modality is necessary to assess the possibility of sarcoma, giant cell tumor, or metastatic disease in pagetic bone?
items.explanation: MRI is necessary to assess the possibility of sarcoma, giant cell tumor, or metastatic disease in pagetic bone.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · PAGET'S DISEASE OF BONE (part 3)

items.question: Which pharmacologic agent is currently recommended as the first choice for treating severe Paget's disease?
items.explanation: Zoledronic acid is recommended as the first choice for severe Paget's disease due to its rapid normalization of bone turnover.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · Paget's Disease of Bone

items.question: What is a contraindication for using zoledronic acid in patients with Paget's disease?
options: Bone pain due to lytic lesion
items.explanation: Zoledronic acid is contraindicated in patients with significant renal impairment, specifically a glomerular filtration rate <35 mL/min.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · Paget's Disease of Bone

items.question: What percentage of patients experience a flulike syndrome after the first infusion of zoledronic acid?
items.explanation: About 20–25% of patients experience a flulike syndrome after the first infusion of zoledronic acid.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · Paget's Disease of Bone

items.question: Which oral bisphosphonates are recommended for subjects with mild Paget's disease or some degree of renal impairment?
items.explanation: Oral bisphosphonates such as alendronate and risedronate are recommended for patients with mild disease or some degree of renal impairment.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · Paget's Disease of Bone

items.question: Which gene mutation is associated with autosomal dominant osteopetrosis type II?
items.explanation: Mutations in the CLCN7 chloride channel gene cause autosomal dominant osteopetrosis type II.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · OSTEOPETROSIS

items.question: What is the primary defect in osteopetrosis?
options: Loss of normal osteoblastic bone formation
items.explanation: The primary defect in osteopetrosis is the impairment of osteoclast-mediated bone resorption.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · OSTEOPETROSIS

items.question: Which condition can result from autosomal recessive carbonic anhydrase (CA) II deficiency?
options: Osteopetrosis of intermediate severity with renal tubular acidosis and cerebral calcification
items.explanation: Autosomal recessive carbonic anhydrase (CA) II deficiency produces osteopetrosis of intermediate severity associated with renal tubular acidosis and cerebral calcification.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · OSTEOPETROSIS

items.question: What is the typical radiographic finding in osteopetrosis?
options: Decreased bone mass with thinning of cortical bone
Generalized symmetric increases in bone mass with thickening of both cortical and trabecular bone
Localized areas of increased bone density without cortical thickening
Uniformly decreased bone density throughout the skeleton
items.explanation: Typically, there are generalized symmetric increases in bone mass with thickening of both cortical and trabecular bone.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · OSTEOPETROSIS

items.question: Which intervention has been most successful in treating osteopetrosis when performed before age 4?
options: Marrow transplantation from nonidentical HLA-matched donors
Surgical decompression of optic or auditory nerve compression
Allogeneic human leukocyte antigen (HLA)-identical bone marrow transplantation
items.explanation: Allogeneic HLA-identical bone marrow transplantation has been successful, especially when performed before age 4.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · Osteopetrosis | PYKNODYSOSTOSIS

items.question: What is a common radiographic finding in pyknodysostosis that differentiates it from osteopetrosis?
options: Generalized increase in bone density with normally shaped long bones
Complete normalization of bone structure post-treatment
items.explanation: Pyknodysostosis shows a generalized increase in bone density but with normally shaped long bones, unlike osteopetrosis.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · Osteopetrosis | PYKNODYSOSTOSIS

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a clinical feature of pyknodysostosis?
options: Frequent fractures with normal life span
items.explanation: Pyknodysostosis does not typically present with anemia, unlike osteopetrosis.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · Osteopetrosis | PYKNODYSOSTOSIS

items.subtopic: PROGRESSIVE DIAPHYSEAL DYSPLASIA | HYPEROSTOSIS CORTICALIS GENERALISATA
items.question: Which chromosome is associated with the genetic defect responsible for progressive diaphyseal dysplasia?
items.explanation: The genetic defect for progressive diaphyseal dysplasia is localized to chromosome 19q13.2.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · PROGRESSIVE DIAPHYSEAL DYSPLASIA | HYPEROSTOSIS CORTICALIS GENERALISATA

items.subtopic: PROGRESSIVE DIAPHYSEAL DYSPLASIA | HYPEROSTOSIS CORTICALIS GENERALISATA
items.question: What is a common presenting symptom of progressive diaphyseal dysplasia?
options: Pain and tenderness in involved areas
items.explanation: Common presenting symptoms of progressive diaphyseal dysplasia include pain and tenderness of the involved areas.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · PROGRESSIVE DIAPHYSEAL DYSPLASIA | HYPEROSTOSIS CORTICALIS GENERALISATA

items.subtopic: PROGRESSIVE DIAPHYSEAL DYSPLASIA | HYPEROSTOSIS CORTICALIS GENERALISATA
items.question: What treatment may relieve bone pain and reverse abnormal bone formation in progressive diaphyseal dysplasia?
items.explanation: Low-dose glucocorticoids may relieve bone pain and reverse abnormal bone formation in progressive diaphyseal dysplasia.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · PROGRESSIVE DIAPHYSEAL DYSPLASIA | HYPEROSTOSIS CORTICALIS GENERALISATA

items.question: Which gene mutation is associated with melorheostosis and involves the MEK1 activity downstream of the RAS pathway?
items.explanation: Mutations in MAP2K1, which increase MEK1 activity downstream of the RAS pathway, are associated with melorheostosis.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · MELORHEOSTOSIS | OSTEOPOIKILOSIS | HEPATITIS C-ASSOCIATED OSTEOSCLEROSIS

items.question: In osteopoikilosis, which bones are typically not involved by the bony lesions?
items.explanation: Osteopoikilosis does not involve the skull, ribs, or vertebrae.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · MELORHEOSTOSIS | OSTEOPOIKILOSIS | HEPATITIS C-ASSOCIATED OSTEOSCLEROSIS

items.question: What is a common feature of hepatitis C-associated osteosclerosis (HCAO) after several years?
options: Diffuse appendicular bone pain and increased bone mass
items.explanation: HCAO is characterized by diffuse appendicular bone pain and a generalized increase in bone mass.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · MELORHEOSTOSIS | OSTEOPOIKILOSIS | HEPATITIS C-ASSOCIATED OSTEOSCLEROSIS

items.question: Which population has the highest prevalence of severe neonatal and infantile forms of hypophosphatasia?
items.explanation: The disease is most common among Mennonites and Hutterites in Canada.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · HYPOPHOSPHATASIA

items.question: What is the inheritance pattern of severe cases of hypophosphatasia?
items.explanation: Severe cases are inherited in an autosomal recessive manner.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · HYPOPHOSPHATASIA

items.question: Which enzyme deficiency causes hypophosphatasia?
items.explanation: The disease is caused by a deficiency of tissue nonspecific (bone/liver/kidney) ALP (TNSALP).
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · HYPOPHOSPHATASIA

items.question: Which treatment was approved in 2015 for perinatal/infantile- and juvenile-onset forms of hypophosphatasia?
items.explanation: In 2015, asfotase alfa was approved as enzyme replacement therapy for these forms.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · HYPOPHOSPHATASIA

items.question: Which radiographic finding is typical for axial osteomalacia?
options: Osteosclerosis due to coarsened trabecular patterns
Increased bone density with normal trabecular pattern
items.explanation: Radiographic findings in axial osteomalacia typically show osteosclerosis due to coarsened trabecular patterns.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · AXIAL OSTEOMALACIA | FIBROGENESIS IMPERFECTA OSSIUM

items.question: What is the primary defect in axial osteomalacia?
options: Defective mineralization due to abnormal serum calcium levels
items.explanation: The primary defect in axial osteomalacia is an acquired defect in osteoblast function.

items.question: Which biochemical abnormality is associated with fibrogenesis imperfecta ossium?
items.explanation: The only biochemical abnormality associated with fibrogenesis imperfecta ossium is elevated ALP.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · AXIAL OSTEOMALACIA | FIBROGENESIS IMPERFECTA OSSIUM

items.question: Which gene mutation is associated with fibrous dysplasia and McCune-Albright syndrome?
options: GNAS1, which encodes the α subunit of the stimulatory G protein (Gα)
BRCA1, involved in DNA repair mechanisms
items.explanation: Activating mutations in the GNAS1 gene are associated with fibrous dysplasia and McCune-Albright syndrome.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · FIBROUS DYSPLASIA AND MCCUNE-ALBRIGHT SYNDROME

items.question: Which of the following is a common radiographic finding in long bones affected by fibrous dysplasia?
options: Osteosclerotic lesions with increased bone density
Well-defined, radiolucent areas with thin cortices and a ground-glass appearance
Uniformly dense lesions without any trabeculation
Extensive calcification of the extracellular matrix
items.explanation: Fibrous dysplastic lesions in long bones typically appear as well-defined, radiolucent areas with a ground-glass appearance.

items.question: Which endocrine disorder is most commonly associated with McCune-Albright syndrome in girls?
items.explanation: The most common endocrinopathy in McCune-Albright syndrome is isosexual pseudoprecocious puberty, particularly in girls.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · FIBROUS DYSPLASIA AND MCCUNE-ALBRIGHT SYNDROME

items.question: What type of skin lesions are associated with McCune-Albright syndrome?
options: Flat, hyper-pigmented skin lesions with rough borders
items.explanation: McCune-Albright syndrome is associated with flat, hyper-pigmented café au lait spots that have rough borders.

items.question: Which treatment has been reported to lead to some clinical improvement in fibrous dysplasia, though may result in hypercalcemia upon discontinuation?

items.question: What is a distinguishing feature of primary hypertrophic osteoarthropathy on radiography?

Hyperphosphatemia and elevated serum 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D levels
Normal phosphate levels with high parathyroid hormone levels

items.question: What is a potential complication of fibrodysplasia ossificans progressiva following local anesthetic injection?

items.explanation: These supplements should be avoided as they may aggravate hypercalcemia and hypercalciuria.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · HYPOPHOSPHATASIA

items.statement: Calcium and vitamin D therapies are effective in treating axial osteomalacia.
items.explanation: Calcium and vitamin D therapies are not effective for axial osteomalacia.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · AXIAL OSTEOMALACIA | FIBROGENESIS IMPERFECTA OSSIUM

items.statement: Spontaneous remission has been reported in some patients with fibrogenesis imperfecta ossium.
items.explanation: Spontaneous remission has indeed been reported in a small number of patients with fibrogenesis imperfecta ossium.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · AXIAL OSTEOMALACIA | FIBROGENESIS IMPERFECTA OSSIUM

items.statement: Radiation therapy is an effective treatment for fibrous dysplasia.
items.explanation: Radiation has proven to be ineffective and increases the risk of malignant transformation in fibrous dysplasia.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · FIBROUS DYSPLASIA AND MCCUNE-ALBRIGHT SYNDROME

items.statement: Hypophosphatemia in patients with extensive polyostotic lesions is related to elevated levels of FGF23.
items.explanation: The hypophosphatemia and phosphaturia are directly related to the levels of fibroblast growth factor 23 (FGF23).
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · FIBROUS DYSPLASIA AND MCCUNE-ALBRIGHT SYNDROME

items.subtopic: Fibrous Dysplasia and MAS | PACHYDERMOPERIOSTOSIS | OSTEOCHONDRODYSPLASIAS
items.statement: Pachydermoperiostosis is characterized by inflammatory synovial fluid.
items.explanation: Pachydermoperiostosis does not have an inflammatory profile in the synovial fluid.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · Fibrous Dysplasia and MAS | PACHYDERMOPERIOSTOSIS | OSTEOCHONDRODYSPLASIAS

items.subtopic: Fibrous Dysplasia and MAS | PACHYDERMOPERIOSTOSIS | OSTEOCHONDRODYSPLASIAS
items.statement: Vosoritide was approved for use in achondrodysplasia in 2021.
items.explanation: Vosoritide, an analog of C-type natriuretic peptide, was approved in 2021 for use in achondrodysplasia.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · Fibrous Dysplasia and MAS | PACHYDERMOPERIOSTOSIS | OSTEOCHONDRODYSPLASIAS

items.subtopic: EXTRASKELETAL (ECTOPIC) CALCIFICATION AND OSSIFICATION | METASTATIC CALCIFICATION | TUMORAL CALCINOSIS
items.statement: Tumoral calcinosis is characterized by true bone formation in soft tissues.
items.explanation: Tumoral calcinosis involves metastatic calcifications, not true bone formation.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · EXTRASKELETAL (ECTOPIC) CALCIFICATION AND OSSIFICATION | METASTATIC CALCIFICATION | TUMORAL CALCINOSIS

items.subtopic: EXTRASKELETAL (ECTOPIC) CALCIFICATION AND OSSIFICATION | METASTATIC CALCIFICATION | TUMORAL CALCINOSIS
items.statement: Metastatic calcification can occur when the serum calcium × phosphate concentration product is greater than 75.
items.explanation: Calcium phosphate precipitation and metastatic calcification are likely when this product exceeds 75.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · EXTRASKELETAL (ECTOPIC) CALCIFICATION AND OSSIFICATION | METASTATIC CALCIFICATION | TUMORAL CALCINOSIS

items.subtopic: Tumoral Calcinosis | DYSTROPHIC CALCIFICATION | ECTOPIC OSSIFICATION | FIBRODYSPLASIA OSSIFICANS PROGRESSIVA
items.statement: Bisphosphonates and glucocorticoids are effective in halting the progression of ossification in fibrodysplasia ossificans progressiva.
items.explanation: Bisphosphonates, glucocorticoids, and a low-calcium diet have largely been ineffective in halting progression of the ossification.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · Tumoral Calcinosis | DYSTROPHIC CALCIFICATION | ECTOPIC OSSIFICATION | FIBRODYSPLASIA OSSIFICANS PROGRESSIVA

items.subtopic: Tumoral Calcinosis | DYSTROPHIC CALCIFICATION | ECTOPIC OSSIFICATION | FIBRODYSPLASIA OSSIFICANS PROGRESSIVA
items.statement: Surgical removal is recommended for treating ectopic bone in fibrodysplasia ossificans progressiva.
items.explanation: Surgical removal of ectopic bone is not recommended because the trauma of surgery may precipitate formation of new areas of heterotopic bone.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · Tumoral Calcinosis | DYSTROPHIC CALCIFICATION | ECTOPIC OSSIFICATION | FIBRODYSPLASIA OSSIFICANS PROGRESSIVA

items.subtopic: Paget's Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone | PAGET'S DISEASE OF BONE (part 1)
items.assertion: A positive family history is found in 15–25% of patients with Paget's disease.
items.reason: When a positive family history is present, it raises the prevalence of the disease seven- to tenfold among first-degree relatives.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; the reason correctly explains the increased prevalence in families.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · Paget's Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone | PAGET'S DISEASE OF BONE (part 1)

items.subtopic: Paget's Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone | PAGET'S DISEASE OF BONE (part 1)
items.assertion: The viral etiology of Paget's disease is confirmed by culturing live virus from pagetic bone.
items.reason: Evidence suggests a viral infection may contribute to the clinical manifestations of Paget's disease.
items.explanation: The assertion is false as there has been an inability to culture a live virus; however, the reason that evidence suggests a potential viral contribution is true.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · Paget's Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone | PAGET'S DISEASE OF BONE (part 1)

items.assertion: Paget's disease is diagnosed often in asymptomatic patients.
items.reason: Elevated ALP levels or abnormal skeletal radiographs are detected during routine tests.
items.explanation: Diagnosis is frequently made due to elevated ALP levels or incidental findings on radiographs.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · PAGET'S DISEASE OF BONE (part 2)

items.assertion: Paget's disease can lead to high-output heart failure.
items.reason: This is caused by a low degree of skeletal involvement and reduced blood flow through the bone.
items.explanation: High-output heart failure occurs due to extensive arteriovenous shunting in heavily involved skeletons, not low involvement.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · PAGET'S DISEASE OF BONE (part 2)

items.assertion: A patient with Paget's disease may present with leg-length discrepancy.
items.reason: Leg-length discrepancy results from deformities of the long bones in Paget's disease.
items.explanation: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · PAGET'S DISEASE OF BONE (part 3)

items.assertion: Serum osteocalcin is recommended for diagnosis and management of Paget's disease.
items.reason: Serum osteocalcin does not always reflect the activity of Paget's disease.
items.explanation: A is true, but R is false; serum osteocalcin is not recommended for use in diagnosis or management.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · PAGET'S DISEASE OF BONE (part 3)

items.assertion: Early therapy in Paget's disease may prevent late complications.
items.reason: The restoration of normal bone architecture following suppression of pagetic activity will likely prevent further deformities and complications.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but whether early therapy prevents late complications remains undetermined.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · Paget's Disease of Bone

items.assertion: Oral bisphosphonates should be taken with food.
items.reason: They need to be absorbed in the presence of gastric acid for efficacy.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; oral bisphosphonates should be taken on an empty stomach, not with food.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · Paget's Disease of Bone

items.assertion: Osteoprotegerin overexpression in transgenic mice leads to osteoporosis.
items.reason: Overexpression of OPG blocks RANK ligand, which is necessary for osteoclast differentiation and activation.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why overexpression of OPG leads to osteoporosis.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · OSTEOPETROSIS

items.assertion: The incidence of autosomal recessive severe (malignant) osteopetrosis is 1 in 10,000 live births.
items.reason: Malignant infantile osteopetrosis results from mutations in the TCIRG1 gene encoding a subunit of the vacuolar proton pump.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the incidence is actually 1 in 200,000 to 1 in 500,000 live births. The reason is true but unrelated to the incorrect incidence rate.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · OSTEOPETROSIS

items.assertion: Hypophosphatasia can present with premature loss of deciduous teeth.
items.reason: Premature tooth loss is the hallmark of childhood hypophosphatasia.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · HYPOPHOSPHATASIA

items.reason: The accumulation of phosphoethanolamine causes hypercalcemia.
items.explanation: While hypercalcemia is common, it is due to defective ALP and not specifically the accumulation of phosphoethanolamine.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · HYPOPHOSPHATASIA

items.assertion: The course of axial osteomalacia is benign.
items.reason: The primary defect in axial osteomalacia is an acquired defect in osteoblast function.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not explain why the course of axial osteomalacia is benign.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · AXIAL OSTEOMALACIA | FIBROGENESIS IMPERFECTA OSSIUM

items.assertion: The skeletal lesions in fibrous dysplasia are composed largely of mesenchymal cells that do not differentiate into osteoblasts.
items.reason: This results in the formation of imperfect bone with areas where fibroblast-like cells develop features of osteoblasts producing extracellular matrix.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why skeletal lesions result in imperfect bone.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · FIBROUS DYSPLASIA AND MCCUNE-ALBRIGHT SYNDROME

items.assertion: The polyostotic form of fibrous dysplasia typically manifests in adults over 30 years old.
items.reason: Early-onset disease is generally more severe and the polyostotic form usually presents in children under 10 years old.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because the polyostotic form typically manifests in children <10 years old, not adults over 30.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · FIBROUS DYSPLASIA AND MCCUNE-ALBRIGHT SYNDROME

items.subtopic: Fibrous Dysplasia and MAS | PACHYDERMOPERIOSTOSIS | OSTEOCHONDRODYSPLASIAS
items.assertion: Enchondromatosis is characterized by the accumulation of cartilage due to its abnormal resorption.
items.reason: The primary cartilage in enchondromatosis is not resorbed normally, leading to cartilage accumulation.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · Fibrous Dysplasia and MAS | PACHYDERMOPERIOSTOSIS | OSTEOCHONDRODYSPLASIAS

items.subtopic: Fibrous Dysplasia and MAS | PACHYDERMOPERIOSTOSIS | OSTEOCHONDRODYSPLASIAS
items.assertion: Multiple osteochondromas result from inactivating mutations of the EXT1 and EXT2 genes.
items.reason: These mutations lead to a deficiency in heparan sulfate, impacting signaling pathways.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · Fibrous Dysplasia and MAS | PACHYDERMOPERIOSTOSIS | OSTEOCHONDRODYSPLASIAS

items.subtopic: EXTRASKELETAL (ECTOPIC) CALCIFICATION AND OSSIFICATION | METASTATIC CALCIFICATION | TUMORAL CALCINOSIS
items.assertion: Tumoral calcinosis is associated with elevated serum phosphate levels.
items.reason: The disease results from gene mutations leading to increased renal tubular reabsorption of phosphate.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains the assertion.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · EXTRASKELETAL (ECTOPIC) CALCIFICATION AND OSSIFICATION | METASTATIC CALCIFICATION | TUMORAL CALCINOSIS

items.subtopic: EXTRASKELETAL (ECTOPIC) CALCIFICATION AND OSSIFICATION | METASTATIC CALCIFICATION | TUMORAL CALCINOSIS
items.assertion: Metastatic calcification is primarily due to hyperphosphatemia.
items.reason: Hypercalcemia alone can cause metastatic calcifications in the kidney, lungs, and gastric mucosa.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; while hyperphosphatemia contributes, hypercalcemia also plays a role.
items.reference: Paget s Disease and Other Dysplasias of Bone · EXTRASKELETAL (ECTOPIC) CALCIFICATION AND OSSIFICATION | METASTATIC CALCIFICATION | TUMORAL CALCINOSIS
